sections.title: Exam Facts and Readiness Standard
items: The NCE is a variable-length computerized adaptive test for entry into nurse anesthesia practice.
Question count ranges from 100 to 170 questions, with a maximum testing time of 3 hours including tutorial time.
Questions are presented one at a time. Once an answer is confirmed, the candidate cannot return to previous questions.
Content weighting: Basic Sciences 20%; Equipment, Instrumentation and Technology 20%; General Principles of Anesthesia 35%; Surgical Procedures and Special Populations 25%.
Passing scaled score is 450 on a 0 to 900 scale. Domain scores are independent and should not be averaged.

Readiness target: two consecutive mixed mock exams at 85% or higher, every major domain at 80% or higher, high-risk domains at 90% or higher, hard questions at 70% or higher, and no repeated critical safety errors.

sections.title: Six-Week Strategy
items: Week 1: Reset diagnostics, basic sciences, physiology, pharmacology, and equipment fundamentals. Build the error log from day one.
Week 2: Airway, monitoring, fluids, blood, regional, and general anesthesia foundations. Take Mock 1 at the end of Week 2.
Week 3: Convert to mixed-domain case practice. Add equipment troubleshooting, preop-to-PACU decisions, and surgical procedure integration.
Week 4: Pressure-test special populations, crisis management, hard questions, and timed performance. Take Mock 2.
Week 5: Remediate weakest two domains and drill airway, pharmacology, monitoring, and physiology.
Week 6: Take Mock 3, review rationales, taper volume, and focus on formulas, safety algorithms, and missed questions.

sections.title: Daily Study Method
items: Use active recall before reading notes. Try to answer the question from memory before looking at options.
Mark confidence before checking the answer: High, Medium, Low, or Guess.
Review every rationale, including wrong-answer rationales, because distractors often contain the teaching point.
Log every miss by domain, subtopic, mistake type, correct reasoning, and retest date.
Retest weak topics within 72 hours. Do not let misses age without retrieval practice.
Practice forward-only commitment because the live NCE does not permit backtracking.

domains.title: Domain I: Basic Sciences (20%)
items: Cardiac output = HR x SV. Stroke volume depends on preload, afterload, and contractility.
MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP). Coronary perfusion pressure = aortic DBP - LVEDP.
Tachycardia shortens diastole and can severely impair coronary filling, especially in CAD and aortic stenosis.
FRC = ERV + RV. FRC decreases with supine positioning, obesity, pregnancy, and anesthesia, increasing apnea desaturation risk.
A-a gradient = PAO2 - PaO2. Elevation suggests V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment.
100% FiO2 may not correct true shunt; V/Q mismatch usually improves with supplemental oxygen.
CPP = MAP - ICP, or MAP - CVP if CVP is higher. CPP below about 50 mmHg risks ischemia.
Cerebral autoregulation generally maintains CBF over MAP 50-150 mmHg in normotensive adults; chronic hypertension shifts the curve right.
PaCO2 strongly controls cerebral blood flow. Hypocapnia decreases CBF and ICP; hypercapnia increases CBF and ICP.
Aortic stenosis goals: sinus rhythm, slow-normal HR, full preload, and maintained SVR. Avoid tachycardia, hypotension, and vasodilation.
Aortic regurgitation goals: faster HR, lower SVR, and adequate preload. Avoid bradycardia and high afterload.
Mitral stenosis goals: low-normal HR, sinus rhythm, moderate preload, and maintained SVR. Avoid tachycardia and atrial fibrillation.
Mitral regurgitation generally benefits from higher HR and lower SVR. Avoid bradycardia and high afterload.
HOCM worsens with reduced preload, reduced SVR, tachycardia, and positive inotropy. Maintain preload, SVR, and sinus rhythm.
Lower blood:gas coefficient means faster inhaled-agent onset and offset. Desflurane and nitrous oxide are fastest.

Succinylcholine contraindications include malignant hyperthermia history, major burns after 24 hours, denervation injuries, crush injury, prolonged immobilization, myopathies, and pseudocholinesterase deficiency.

domains.title: Domain II: Equipment, Instrumentation and Technology (20%)
items: Oxygen pipeline pressure is usually about 50 psi. Oxygen E-cylinder pressure when full is about 2200 psi with about 660 L available.
E-cylinder oxygen estimate: liters available = psi x 0.3; duration = liters available / flow rate.
Fail-safe valves reduce or interrupt nitrous oxide when oxygen supply pressure falls, but they do not prevent hypoxic mixtures from all causes.
Oxygen analyzers are the final defense against hypoxic gas delivery; check them during machine checkout.
Vaporizers are agent-specific, temperature compensated, and concentration calibrated. Filling with the wrong agent can cause dangerous output error.
Desflurane requires a heated, pressurized vaporizer because of its high vapor pressure and low boiling point.
Circle-system rebreathing can occur with exhausted CO2 absorbent, incompetent unidirectional valves, or inadequate fresh gas flow during some configurations.
Capnography patterns are high-yield: obstruction creates a slanted upstroke, rebreathing raises baseline, esophageal intubation rapidly loses waveform, and embolism can abruptly reduce ETCO2.
Pulse oximetry can lag during rapid desaturation, especially with poor perfusion. Treat waveform quality as part of the reading.
Peripheral nerve stimulator interpretation requires correct electrode placement, current, and understanding of train-of-four fade.

domains.title: Domain III: General Principles of Anesthesia (35%)
items: Airway planning must include Plan A, Plan B, Plan C, and rescue oxygenation before induction.
Rapid sequence induction prioritizes preoxygenation, aspiration-risk mitigation, rapid paralysis, and a prepared failed-airway pathway.
Do not let a difficult intubation become a failed oxygenation event. Oxygenation is the endpoint that matters.
Preoxygenation is most vulnerable in pregnancy, obesity, sepsis, pediatric patients, and patients with reduced FRC or increased oxygen consumption.
Hypotension after induction often reflects vasodilation, myocardial depression, relative hypovolemia, or sympathetic withdrawal. Treat the cause, not only the number.
Balanced anesthesia combines hypnosis, amnesia, analgesia, akinesia, autonomic control, and physiologic stability.
Regional anesthesia risks include sympathectomy, local anesthetic systemic toxicity, high spinal, nerve injury, bleeding, and infection.
LAST treatment centers on airway support, seizure control, ACLS modifications, and lipid emulsion therapy.
Massive transfusion decisions should consider ongoing bleeding, hemodynamics, labs, temperature, calcium, acid-base status, and coagulation testing when available.
Malignant hyperthermia clues include rising ETCO2, tachycardia, rigidity, acidosis, hyperkalemia, and hyperthermia as a later sign. Treat with dantrolene and trigger removal.

domains.title: Domain IV: Surgical Procedures and Special Populations (25%)
items: Pregnancy decreases FRC and increases oxygen consumption, causing rapid desaturation during apnea.
Aortocaval compression can reduce venous return in late pregnancy. Use left uterine displacement when appropriate.
Pediatric patients have higher oxygen consumption and lower reserve; bradycardia is often hypoxia until proven otherwise.
Older adults often require lower anesthetic doses and are more vulnerable to hypotension, delirium, hypothermia, and drug accumulation.
Obesity decreases FRC, increases difficult-airway risk, and may require ramped positioning for preoxygenation and intubation.
Laparoscopy can decrease venous return, increase SVR, raise PaCO2, and worsen respiratory mechanics.
One-lung ventilation requires confirmation of lung isolation, oxygenation strategy, and awareness that volatile anesthetics can blunt HPV.
Neurosurgical anesthesia emphasizes ICP control, cerebral perfusion, smooth airway management, and avoidance of hypoxia, hypercarbia, and hypotension.
Tourniquet release can cause transient hypotension, ETCO2 changes, potassium and lactate increase, and temperature decrease. Anticipate and support hemodynamics.
Special-population questions usually test physiology applied to anesthetic priorities, not isolated memorization.
